text: When a field sales agent prepares for a client meeting, how complete and accessible is the information they need?
preAnswerContext: This question tells us whether your sales process is supported by the right information infrastructure. A low score here almost always means one of three things: the data exists but isn't integrated, the process doesn't mandate preparation, or nobody owns the data quality. Your answer directly feeds the 'Customer & Revenue — Data Access' diagnosis.

anchors.label: No preparation
anchors.description: Agents go in with no structured information; it's entirely from memory

anchors.label: Partial, manual
anchors.description: Some info exists but requires manual searching across multiple tools

anchors.label: Accessible but incomplete
anchors.description: A CRM record exists but misses recent interactions or context

anchors.label: Mostly complete
anchors.description: Most info is in one place; minor gaps or occasional staleness

anchors.label: Complete and real-time
anchors.description: Full context auto-surfaced before every meeting, zero manual effort

text: After a sales meeting ends, what happens to the insights, commitments, and next steps that were discussed?
preAnswerContext: This question reveals whether your organisation captures the value generated during sales conversations or lets it evaporate. A low score typically indicates a dark process problem — critical commercial intelligence lives only in someone's head or in scattered notes. This feeds the 'Customer & Revenue — Knowledge Capture' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Nothing captured
anchors.description: Meeting outcomes stay in the rep's memory; no notes, no CRM update, no follow-up trail

anchors.label: Informal notes
anchors.description: Some reps jot notes in personal notebooks or emails, but nothing enters the system consistently

anchors.label: CRM updated sporadically
anchors.description: CRM entries are made but are often delayed by days, incomplete, or inconsistent across reps

anchors.label: Structured capture
anchors.description: A defined post-meeting process exists and most reps follow it within 24 hours with key fields populated

anchors.label: Real-time structured logging
anchors.description: Meeting notes, action items, and next steps are captured during or immediately after the meeting in a structured format that auto-populates CRM and triggers workflows

text: How does your sales team decide which prospects to prioritise on any given day or week?
preAnswerContext: This question diagnoses whether prioritisation is driven by evidence or by instinct. When reps choose their own focus without data-backed guidance, effort allocation becomes inconsistent and high-value opportunities get equal time as low-probability ones. This feeds the 'Customer & Revenue — Prioritisation' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Gut feel only
anchors.description: Reps choose who to call based on personal relationships, recency, or whoever comes to mind first

anchors.label: Manager direction
anchors.description: Managers assign focus accounts in weekly meetings, but the criteria are subjective and change frequently

anchors.label: Basic segmentation
anchors.description: Accounts are segmented by size or industry, but within segments there is no further scoring or ranking

anchors.label: Scoring model exists
anchors.description: A lead or opportunity scoring model is in place and most reps reference it, though overrides are common

anchors.label: Dynamic prioritisation
anchors.description: An automated system surfaces daily prioritised lists based on engagement signals, deal stage, and predicted close probability

text: When a sales deal stalls or goes quiet, how does your organisation detect and respond to that?
preAnswerContext: Stalled deals are one of the largest hidden revenue leaks in B2B sales. This question reveals whether your organisation has systematic detection of pipeline stagnation or relies on individual reps to self-report problems. A low score here often pairs with pipeline management gaps to indicate a manual bottleneck pattern.

anchors.label: Undetected
anchors.description: Stalled deals are only noticed when a quarterly review reveals unchanged pipeline stages from months ago

anchors.label: Manager-driven review
anchors.description: Managers occasionally ask about dormant deals in 1:1s, but there is no systematic tracking of deal velocity

anchors.label: CRM age reports
anchors.description: Standard reports show deals that haven't moved stage in X days, but reviewing and acting on them is manual

anchors.label: Alerts and playbooks
anchors.description: Automated alerts fire when deals stall, and there are documented re-engagement playbooks that reps are expected to follow

anchors.label: Predictive intervention
anchors.description: The system predicts stall risk before it happens based on engagement patterns and auto-triggers tailored re-engagement sequences

text: How well does your organisation understand why customers buy from you versus your competitors?
preAnswerContext: Win/loss intelligence is the foundation of competitive positioning and sales enablement. This question reveals whether your customer intelligence is structured and actionable or anecdotal. A pattern of low scores here alongside higher process scores often indicates an insight-action gap — you may have data but aren't extracting strategic meaning from it.

anchors.label: Anecdotal at best
anchors.description: Win/loss reasons are based on individual rep opinions shared informally; no structured data collection exists

anchors.label: Exit surveys only
anchors.description: Lost deals sometimes get a follow-up survey, but response rates are low and results are not systematically analysed

anchors.label: Periodic analysis
anchors.description: Win/loss analysis is done quarterly or semi-annually, producing reports that inform strategy discussions

anchors.label: Structured programme
anchors.description: A formal win/loss programme interviews buyers, codes reasons consistently, and feeds findings into sales training and positioning

anchors.label: Continuous intelligence loop
anchors.description: Win/loss data is captured in real time, enriched with market signals, and automatically updates competitive battlecards and messaging

text: If a key customer's buying behaviour changed significantly last month, how quickly would your team know?
preAnswerContext: This question measures your organisation's ability to detect customer signals — both risk and opportunity — in near real time. A delayed response here often indicates that customer data is siloed across billing, support, and sales systems with no unified view. This is a strong indicator of integration maturity.

anchors.label: Wouldn't know
anchors.description: Changes in buying patterns would go unnoticed until a contract renewal conversation or annual review

anchors.label: Months later
anchors.description: Finance might flag a revenue variance in monthly reporting, but the insight rarely reaches the account team quickly

anchors.label: Weeks later via reports
anchors.description: Standard dashboards show customer trends but are reviewed periodically, not proactively monitored

anchors.label: Days via alerts
anchors.description: Threshold-based alerts notify account managers when key metrics shift beyond normal ranges

anchors.label: Same day, contextualised
anchors.description: Real-time monitoring detects behavioural changes, enriches them with context from multiple systems, and surfaces actionable insights to the account team immediately

text: How does your team build a picture of what a customer needs next — before the customer asks?
preAnswerContext: Proactive customer intelligence separates reactive order-takers from strategic partners. This question diagnoses whether your organisation can anticipate customer needs using data patterns or relies entirely on customers to articulate their own requirements. A high score here is a prerequisite for genuine AI candidacy in the customer domain.

anchors.label: Entirely reactive
anchors.description: The team waits for inbound requests or RFPs; no systematic effort to anticipate needs

anchors.label: Relationship-based guessing
anchors.description: Experienced account managers use personal knowledge to guess what clients might need, but it's not data-driven

anchors.label: Segment-level patterns
anchors.description: Marketing or product teams identify broad segment trends, but these rarely translate into specific account-level actions

anchors.label: Account-level signals
anchors.description: Usage data, support patterns, and engagement history are reviewed per account to identify likely next needs

anchors.label: Predictive recommendations
anchors.description: Models analyse cross-customer patterns to predict account-specific needs, generating proactive outreach recommendations with timing and messaging

text: How accurate is your revenue forecast at 30, 60, and 90 days out?
preAnswerContext: Forecast accuracy is a litmus test for pipeline health, data quality, and process discipline. Consistently inaccurate forecasts almost always trace back to either unreliable pipeline data (a data trust problem) or inconsistent qualification standards (a process consistency problem). This is one of the highest-signal questions in the CRV domain.

anchors.label: Unreliable
anchors.description: Forecasts are routinely off by more than 30%; leadership treats them as directional at best

anchors.label: Wide variance
anchors.description: Accuracy varies wildly by team or region; some managers forecast well, others are consistently off by 20%+

anchors.label: Reasonable at 30 days
anchors.description: 30-day forecasts are within 15% but 60- and 90-day accuracy degrades significantly

anchors.label: Reliable with caveats
anchors.description: Forecasts are within 10% at 30 days and 15% at 90 days; methodology is documented and followed by most teams

anchors.label: High confidence all horizons
anchors.description: Forecast accuracy is within 5-10% across all time horizons, supported by weighted pipeline models and regular inspection cadences

text: How many handoffs happen between a customer saying 'yes' and revenue being recognised — and where do things break?
preAnswerContext: The quote-to-cash process is where revenue operations maturity becomes visible. Excessive handoffs, manual steps, and system gaps between sales close and revenue recognition create delays, errors, and customer friction. A low score here typically reveals process fragmentation and automation opportunities.

anchors.label: Chaotic and manual
anchors.description: Multiple handoffs via email or spreadsheet; deals regularly get stuck between teams for days, with no visibility into where they are

anchors.label: Defined but fragile
anchors.description: The process is documented somewhere, but it involves 5+ manual steps across disconnected systems and frequently breaks down

anchors.label: Partially automated
anchors.description: Some steps are automated (e.g., CRM to billing), but key transitions still require manual intervention and error-checking

anchors.label: Mostly streamlined
anchors.description: End-to-end process is largely automated with 1-2 manual checkpoints; exceptions are handled through defined workflows

anchors.label: Seamless and tracked
anchors.description: Quote-to-cash flows through integrated systems with full visibility, automated approvals, and real-time status tracking for all stakeholders

text: When you look at your revenue data, how confident are you that the numbers across your CRM, billing, and finance systems actually agree?
preAnswerContext: Data consistency across revenue systems is a foundational requirement for everything from forecasting to compensation. When CRM, billing, and finance report different numbers, teams lose trust in all of them and fall back on manual reconciliation. This question directly tests data trust and system integration health.

anchors.label: Contradictory
anchors.description: Different systems show different revenue numbers; teams maintain their own shadow spreadsheets as the 'real' source

anchors.label: Manual reconciliation
anchors.description: Someone spends hours each month reconciling CRM, billing, and finance data; discrepancies are found every cycle

anchors.label: Mostly aligned
anchors.description: Systems broadly agree but there are known gaps — e.g., CRM doesn't capture amendments, or billing lags by a week

anchors.label: Consistent with minor lags
anchors.description: Data flows between systems are automated; minor timing differences exist but are understood and documented

anchors.label: Single source of truth
anchors.description: All revenue systems sync in real time with automated validation checks; discrepancies are flagged and resolved immediately

text: Walk me through how a new opportunity enters your pipeline — from first qualification to the point where it has a value and expected close date.
preAnswerContext: Pipeline creation discipline determines the quality of everything downstream — forecasting, resource allocation, and win-rate analysis. This question reveals whether your pipeline process is standardised or whether every rep has their own definition of 'qualified.' Inconsistency here is one of the most common root causes of revenue unpredictability.

anchors.label: No standard process
anchors.description: Reps add opportunities whenever they feel like it; no qualification criteria, inconsistent data entry, many stale or junk records

anchors.label: Loose guidelines
anchors.description: There are qualification guidelines but adherence varies widely; deal values are often placeholder numbers

anchors.label: Defined stages
anchors.description: Pipeline stages are defined with entry criteria; most reps follow them, but data quality still varies by team

anchors.label: Enforced process
anchors.description: Qualification methodology is enforced through CRM validation rules; deals cannot advance without meeting stage-gate criteria

anchors.label: Data-validated pipeline
anchors.description: Pipeline entries are validated against historical patterns; system flags unrealistic values, missing fields, or stage-skipping automatically

text: How does your leadership team currently review pipeline health — and what actions typically result from that review?
preAnswerContext: Pipeline reviews are where data meets decision-making. This question reveals whether pipeline reviews drive action or are merely reporting rituals. A common pattern is that organisations have sophisticated pipeline data but the review process doesn't produce specific, tracked interventions — the classic insight-action gap.

anchors.label: No structured review
anchors.description: Pipeline is discussed informally; no regular cadence, no standard metrics, actions are ad hoc suggestions

anchors.label: Monthly reporting
anchors.description: A monthly pipeline report is circulated; leadership discusses it but specific actions are rarely documented or tracked

anchors.label: Weekly reviews
anchors.description: Weekly pipeline reviews use standard dashboards; actions are assigned but follow-through tracking is manual

anchors.label: Structured inspection
anchors.description: Regular reviews follow a defined methodology; deal-level actions are logged, assigned, and tracked to completion

anchors.label: Continuous, data-triggered
anchors.description: Pipeline health is monitored continuously with automated alerts; reviews focus on exceptions and strategic interventions rather than status reporting

text: If I asked three different sales managers to estimate the probability of closing their top five deals, how consistent would their criteria be?
preAnswerContext: Deal probability estimation is one of the clearest tests of process consistency. When managers apply different mental models to the same question, the aggregate pipeline becomes unreliable. This question specifically tests for the inconsistency-signal pattern and often reveals whether qualification frameworks are genuinely adopted or only nominal.

anchors.label: Completely inconsistent
anchors.description: Each manager uses entirely different criteria; probabilities are essentially personal confidence levels with no shared framework

anchors.label: Nominally aligned
anchors.description: A qualification framework exists on paper (e.g., MEDDIC, BANT) but managers interpret and apply it very differently

anchors.label: Broadly similar
anchors.description: Most managers reference the same criteria but weight them differently; a '70% deal' means different things to different people

anchors.label: Calibrated
anchors.description: Regular calibration sessions ensure managers apply consistent criteria; historical accuracy is tracked per manager

anchors.label: Data-validated consistency
anchors.description: Probability estimates are system-generated based on objective stage-gate criteria and validated against historical close rates

text: When a new customer signs a contract, what does the handoff from sales to implementation or customer success look like?
preAnswerContext: The sales-to-onboarding handoff is where customer promises meet operational reality. A poor handoff means implementation teams re-discover requirements, customers repeat themselves, and time-to-value stretches. This question reveals whether your handoff is a structured transfer of knowledge or an information cliff.

anchors.label: No formal handoff
anchors.description: Implementation teams discover the customer from scratch; sales context is lost entirely, customer repeats everything

anchors.label: Email-based handoff
anchors.description: Sales sends a summary email to the onboarding team; quality and completeness vary entirely by rep

anchors.label: Handoff meeting
anchors.description: A structured handoff meeting occurs with a standard template, but key details still fall through the cracks regularly

anchors.label: Documented transfer
anchors.description: A comprehensive handoff document is completed in the CRM before the deal is marked closed; onboarding team reviews and confirms

anchors.label: Seamless transition
anchors.description: All sales context, requirements, commitments, and customer preferences automatically flow into the onboarding system; customer experiences zero knowledge loss

text: How long does it take a new customer to reach the point where they're getting real value from what they bought — and do you measure that?
preAnswerContext: Time-to-value is the single most predictive metric for customer retention and expansion. This question reveals whether your organisation even tracks this milestone and whether the onboarding process is designed to accelerate it. A lack of measurement here is itself a diagnostic signal — you cannot improve what you do not observe.

anchors.label: Not measured
anchors.description: There is no definition of 'first value' and no tracking of onboarding duration; the concept is not formalised

anchors.label: Anecdotally known
anchors.description: Teams have a general sense of how long onboarding takes, but there is no consistent metric or milestone definition

anchors.label: Tracked per project
anchors.description: Onboarding timelines are tracked at the project level, but there is no aggregate view or benchmark across customers

anchors.label: KPI with target
anchors.description: Time-to-value is a defined KPI with a target; it is tracked per customer and reviewed in operations meetings

anchors.label: Optimised and predictive
anchors.description: Time-to-value is measured automatically, benchmarked by segment, and onboarding processes are continuously optimised to reduce it; at-risk onboardings are flagged early

text: How does your organisation decide on pricing for a new deal — and how much discretion does an individual sales rep have?
preAnswerContext: Pricing discipline directly impacts margin, deal velocity, and customer equity. This question reveals whether pricing is governed by clear rules, managed through approval workflows, or left to individual negotiation. Excessive discretion without guardrails is a common automation-ready pattern — rules exist but are enforced manually.

anchors.label: Fully ad hoc
anchors.description: Reps set pricing case by case with no standard list or guidelines; similar customers get very different prices

anchors.label: Price list with wide overrides
anchors.description: A price list exists but reps frequently deviate; discount approval is informal or routinely granted

anchors.label: Guided with approval gates
anchors.description: Standard pricing tiers exist with defined discount thresholds that trigger manager approval

anchors.label: Rules-based with exceptions
anchors.description: Pricing is largely automated through CPQ or rules engines; exceptions go through a structured approval workflow

anchors.label: Dynamic and optimised
anchors.description: Pricing is dynamically calculated based on customer segment, deal characteristics, and competitive context; optimisation models suggest optimal price points

text: When a customer asks for a custom commercial arrangement — a non-standard contract, bundled pricing, or special terms — how does that request flow through your organisation?
preAnswerContext: Non-standard commercial requests are a stress test for your revenue operations maturity. They reveal how well sales, legal, finance, and delivery coordinate on exceptions. A slow or opaque process here often signals manual bottlenecks — too many approvals routed through too few people with too little system support.

anchors.label: Chaotic
anchors.description: Custom requests are handled through informal conversations; no defined process, decisions are inconsistent, and turnaround is unpredictable

anchors.label: Email chains
anchors.description: Requests are routed via email to various approvers; the process is slow, opaque to the rep, and frequently requires chasing

anchors.label: Defined workflow
anchors.description: A documented approval workflow exists with clear roles, but it is still largely manual and can take weeks for complex requests

anchors.label: Streamlined process
anchors.description: Custom requests flow through a structured system with SLAs; most are resolved within days, and the rep has visibility into status

anchors.label: Automated with guardrails
anchors.description: Standard exception types are auto-approved within pre-set parameters; only truly novel arrangements require human review, and those are fast-tracked through a dedicated team

text: How does your account management team decide which existing customers to invest more time in — and which are being under-served?
preAnswerContext: Account prioritisation for existing customers is where retention, expansion, and resource allocation intersect. This question reveals whether your account management is driven by data-informed segmentation or by relationship inertia. A common gap is that account managers spend disproportionate time on vocal clients rather than high-potential or at-risk ones.

anchors.label: Relationship-driven
anchors.description: Account managers focus on whoever calls them or whoever they have the best relationship with; no data-driven prioritisation

anchors.label: Revenue-based tiers
anchors.description: Accounts are tiered by current revenue, but there is no assessment of growth potential, risk, or engagement depth

anchors.label: Multi-factor segmentation
anchors.description: Accounts are segmented by revenue, strategic value, and growth potential; account plans exist for top-tier accounts

anchors.label: Health-scored
anchors.description: A customer health score combines usage, support, engagement, and commercial data to identify at-risk and high-growth accounts

anchors.label: Predictive portfolio management
anchors.description: Account prioritisation is dynamically updated based on predictive models that factor in propensity to expand, churn risk, and lifetime value trajectory

text: When a customer shows signs of dissatisfaction or disengagement, how does your organisation detect and respond to that?
preAnswerContext: Churn prevention starts with detection speed. This question tests whether your organisation can identify at-risk customers before they raise a formal complaint or simply leave. Late detection is almost always a system integration issue — the signals exist across support tickets, usage data, and billing patterns, but nobody is connecting them.

anchors.label: After they leave
anchors.description: Dissatisfaction is only discovered when the customer cancels, doesn't renew, or sends a formal complaint

anchors.label: Reactive escalation
anchors.description: Issues surface through support escalations or angry emails; the account manager then scrambles to intervene

anchors.label: Periodic check-ins
anchors.description: Account managers conduct regular business reviews and NPS surveys, catching some issues, but many signals slip through between touchpoints

anchors.label: Multi-signal monitoring
anchors.description: Support data, usage trends, and engagement metrics are combined into a health score that flags at-risk accounts for proactive outreach

anchors.label: Predictive and proactive
anchors.description: Machine learning models identify disengagement patterns weeks before they become visible, triggering automated and personalised retention plays

text: How effectively does your organisation identify and execute cross-sell or upsell opportunities within your existing customer base?
preAnswerContext: Expansion revenue from existing customers is typically the highest-margin growth source, yet most organisations leave it to individual account managers' initiative. This question reveals whether customer expansion is systematic or opportunistic. A strong score here combined with weak scores in customer intelligence questions indicates automation readiness — the process works but doesn't scale.

anchors.label: Opportunistic only
anchors.description: Cross-sell happens when an account manager happens to notice something or a customer asks; no proactive identification process

anchors.label: Annual planning
anchors.description: Account plans identify expansion targets once a year, but execution is left to the individual manager with no supporting data or triggers

anchors.label: Segment-based campaigns
anchors.description: Marketing runs broad cross-sell campaigns by customer segment, but targeting is not account-specific or personalised

anchors.label: Data-informed targeting
anchors.description: Usage and engagement data identifies likely expansion candidates; account managers receive specific recommendations with supporting evidence

anchors.label: Predictive and automated
anchors.description: Models predict which customers are ready for which additional products, generating personalised expansion plays with optimal timing and messaging

text: Walk me through how a production plan or fulfilment schedule gets created each week — who is involved, what inputs do they use, and how long does it take?
preAnswerContext: Production planning is the heartbeat of operations. This question reveals whether your planning process is structured and data-driven or whether it relies on tribal knowledge and manual coordination. A fragmented planning process is one of the strongest indicators that foundational process discipline is missing before any technology intervention makes sense.

anchors.label: Ad hoc and informal
anchors.description: Planning happens in hallway conversations and personal spreadsheets; no standard cadence, no single owner, inputs change every week

anchors.label: Manual coordination
anchors.description: A planner compiles data from emails and spreadsheets into a weekly plan, but the process takes hours and frequently changes mid-cycle

anchors.label: Template-driven
anchors.description: A standard planning template exists and is followed most weeks; inputs come from defined sources but data extraction is still manual

anchors.label: System-supported
anchors.description: Planning software pulls demand and capacity data automatically; the planner focuses on exceptions and adjustments rather than data gathering

anchors.label: Optimised and dynamic
anchors.description: Plans are generated algorithmically from real-time demand, capacity, and constraint data; human planners focus on strategic trade-offs and the plan auto-adjusts to changes

text: When demand changes unexpectedly — a rush order, a cancellation, a spike — how quickly can your operations team adjust the plan?
preAnswerContext: Demand volatility is inevitable; the question is how your planning process absorbs it. A slow response to demand changes usually points to rigid planning systems, manual replanning effort, or poor visibility into current capacity. This directly measures your planning agility and feeds the automation-readiness assessment.

anchors.label: Days or longer
anchors.description: Plan changes require a full replanning cycle; rush orders cause chaos and the team scrambles without a structured response

anchors.label: Same day with effort
anchors.description: The planner can adjust within a day but it requires multiple phone calls, manual recalculations, and cascading updates to other teams

anchors.label: Hours with defined process
anchors.description: A change management process exists; adjustments take a few hours and affected teams are notified through a standard workflow

anchors.label: Near real-time
anchors.description: Planning systems recalculate automatically when demand inputs change; the planner reviews and approves adjustments within an hour

anchors.label: Continuous and autonomous
anchors.description: The system continuously re-optimises plans as demand signals change, auto-adjusting schedules and notifying affected parties without manual intervention

text: How well does your production or fulfilment plan account for real constraints — machine capacity, staff availability, supplier lead times, and maintenance windows?
preAnswerContext: Plans that ignore real-world constraints produce schedules that fail on contact with reality. This question diagnoses whether your planning incorporates the full picture or operates on assumptions that create downstream firefighting. When constraint data lives in different systems or people's heads, you see the siloed-systems pattern clearly.

anchors.label: Constraints ignored
anchors.description: Plans are built on theoretical capacity; real constraints like machine downtime or staff absences are discovered only when things break

anchors.label: Known informally
anchors.description: The planner mentally accounts for some constraints based on experience, but none are systematically tracked or integrated into the plan

anchors.label: Partially integrated
anchors.description: Major constraints like machine capacity are factored in, but staff availability and supplier lead times are checked manually and sometimes missed

anchors.label: Mostly integrated
anchors.description: Most constraint data feeds into the planning system; exceptions are flagged automatically, though some inputs still require manual updates

anchors.label: Fully constraint-aware
anchors.description: All constraints — capacity, labour, materials, maintenance — are integrated in real time; the plan is always feasible as produced and auto-flags conflicts

text: If you could look at your production or fulfilment performance over the past three months and identify one pattern that keeps recurring, what would it be?
preAnswerContext: This open-ended question surfaces the pain the client feels most acutely in their operations. The pattern they name — whether it is delays, rework, stockouts, or bottlenecks — tells us where diagnostic attention should focus. It also reveals whether they can see patterns at all, which is itself a test of their analytics maturity.

anchors.label: Cannot identify patterns
anchors.description: No data is collected or reviewed in a way that would reveal recurring issues; problems feel random and unpredictable

anchors.label: Anecdotal awareness
anchors.description: People on the floor know what keeps going wrong, but there is no data to confirm or quantify the pattern

anchors.label: Reported but not actioned
anchors.description: Monthly reports show recurring issues, but the same problems appear month after month without systematic root-cause resolution

anchors.label: Tracked and addressed
anchors.description: Recurring patterns are identified through data, root causes are investigated, and corrective actions are implemented and monitored

anchors.label: Predicted and prevented
anchors.description: Analytics identify emerging patterns before they become problems; preventive actions are triggered automatically based on leading indicators

text: At any given moment, how confident are you that you know where your key materials, components, or goods are in the supply chain?
preAnswerContext: Supply chain visibility is the foundation for planning accuracy, exception management, and customer commitments. When you cannot see where things are, every downstream process compensates with buffers, phone calls, and guesswork. This question directly tests whether your supply chain operates on data or on faith.

anchors.label: Blind
anchors.description: Once a purchase order is sent, there is no systematic way to track where materials are until they arrive — or do not arrive

anchors.label: Periodic updates
anchors.description: Suppliers send status updates by email on request; someone manually tracks key orders in a spreadsheet

anchors.label: Portal-based tracking
anchors.description: Major suppliers offer portals or tracking numbers, but the data is fragmented across systems and not consolidated in one view

anchors.label: Consolidated dashboard
anchors.description: A supply chain dashboard shows status of key orders across suppliers; updates are mostly automated with some manual gaps

anchors.label: Real-time end-to-end
anchors.description: Full supply chain visibility from supplier dispatch to warehouse receipt, with real-time tracking, automated alerts for delays, and integrated into planning systems

text: When a supplier misses a delivery date or delivers the wrong quantity, how does your team find out and what happens next?
preAnswerContext: Supplier reliability management reveals the maturity of your supply chain exception handling. Discovering problems late — at the point of need rather than at the point of shipment — cascades into production delays and customer disappointments. This question tests both detection speed and response discipline.

anchors.label: Discovered at the dock
anchors.description: Problems are only found when goods fail to arrive or are counted at receiving; by then it is too late to mitigate

anchors.label: Manual follow-up
anchors.description: Procurement staff chase suppliers by phone or email when deliveries are overdue; there is no proactive alerting system

anchors.label: Exception alerts
anchors.description: The system flags overdue orders automatically, but the response — finding alternatives, rescheduling — is still manual

anchors.label: Integrated response
anchors.description: Supplier delays trigger automatic alerts to planning and operations teams; predefined mitigation workflows are initiated

anchors.label: Predictive and pre-emptive
anchors.description: The system predicts likely delivery issues based on supplier patterns and logistics data, triggering mitigation actions before a delay occurs

text: How do you evaluate and compare supplier performance over time — and does that evaluation actually influence procurement decisions?
preAnswerContext: Supplier performance management is where operational data should drive strategic sourcing decisions. This question reveals whether your organisation systematically tracks supplier reliability and uses that data to improve outcomes, or whether sourcing decisions are based on relationships and inertia. A gap between measurement and action is the classic insight-action gap.

anchors.label: Not evaluated
anchors.description: Supplier performance is not tracked in any structured way; decisions are based on price quotes and personal relationships

anchors.label: Informal reputation
anchors.description: Procurement staff have opinions about supplier quality based on experience, but no data is collected or reviewed systematically

anchors.label: Scorecards exist
anchors.description: Supplier scorecards are maintained with on-time delivery and quality metrics, but they are updated manually and reviewed infrequently

anchors.label: Regular reviews with consequences
anchors.description: Quarterly supplier reviews use objective performance data; underperformers are put on improvement plans or replaced

anchors.label: Dynamic performance management
anchors.description: Supplier performance is tracked in real time, benchmarked across the supplier base, and automatically factored into sourcing algorithms and order allocation

text: If one of your critical suppliers went offline tomorrow, how quickly could you identify alternatives and switch — and do you know which suppliers are actually critical?
preAnswerContext: Supply chain resilience depends on knowing your single points of failure before they fail. This question tests both your risk awareness and your ability to respond. Organisations that score low here typically have concentration risk they have never mapped and no contingency playbooks. This is a genuine AI candidacy test — predicting and simulating supply chain risk is where AI adds real value.

anchors.label: No contingency
anchors.description: Critical suppliers are not formally identified; if one failed, the team would start from scratch to find alternatives

anchors.label: Known but unplanned
anchors.description: People know which suppliers are critical, but there are no documented alternatives or contingency plans

anchors.label: Backup list exists
anchors.description: Alternative suppliers are identified for critical materials, but they have not been pre-qualified or tested

anchors.label: Qualified alternatives ready
anchors.description: Pre-qualified backup suppliers exist for critical inputs; switching protocols are documented and tested periodically

anchors.label: Resilience modelled
anchors.description: Supply chain risk is continuously modelled; disruption simulations are run regularly, and automated switching protocols activate based on risk triggers

text: How does your team decide what to stock and how much — and how often does reality diverge from those decisions?
preAnswerContext: Inventory planning sits at the intersection of demand forecasting, supply reliability, and financial discipline. This question reveals whether your stocking decisions are driven by data and logic or by habit and fear. Overstocking and stockouts are both symptoms — the root cause is usually either poor demand visibility or a broken feedback loop between sales and operations.

anchors.label: Gut feel and buffer
anchors.description: Stocking levels are based on intuition or historical inertia; excess inventory is the default safety net for uncertainty

anchors.label: Reorder points only
anchors.description: Simple min/max reorder points are set but rarely updated; they do not account for seasonality, trends, or lead time changes

anchors.label: Forecast-informed
anchors.description: Demand forecasts influence stocking decisions, but forecast accuracy is inconsistent and the planning process is largely manual

anchors.label: Data-driven with reviews
anchors.description: Stocking policies are set using statistical methods and reviewed regularly; forecast accuracy is tracked and improved

anchors.label: Dynamically optimised
anchors.description: Inventory levels adjust continuously based on real-time demand signals, lead time variability, and service level targets; the system balances cost and availability automatically

text: How accurate is your inventory data — and when was the last time a team member discovered that what the system said was in stock did not match what was physically on the shelf?
preAnswerContext: Inventory accuracy is a foundational data quality issue. When the system says you have 50 units and the shelf has 30, every downstream process — from order promising to production planning — operates on false premises. This question tests whether your inventory data is trustworthy enough to build decisions on, or whether your team has learned to distrust it.

anchors.label: Frequently wrong
anchors.description: Physical counts regularly reveal major discrepancies; the team does not trust inventory data and checks physically before committing

anchors.label: Known inaccuracies
anchors.description: Certain product lines or locations are known to be unreliable; periodic cycle counts correct major errors but they recur

anchors.label: Mostly accurate
anchors.description: System accuracy is around 90-95%; discrepancies are found occasionally but are corrected through regular cycle counts

anchors.label: High accuracy maintained
anchors.description: Inventory accuracy exceeds 97%; real-time scanning or tracking keeps data current, with exception-based auditing

anchors.label: Near-perfect and trusted
anchors.description: Accuracy exceeds 99%; automated tracking systems maintain real-time precision, and the entire organisation trusts and relies on the data

text: When an item is approaching its expiry date, reaching obsolescence, or has been sitting in stock far longer than it should — how does your team find out?
preAnswerContext: Slow-moving and obsolete inventory is a silent cost drain. This question reveals whether your organisation proactively manages inventory aging or discovers write-offs at year-end. The detection method — whether automated, report-driven, or purely accidental — tells us about both your data capture maturity and your process discipline around inventory lifecycle management.

anchors.label: Discovered accidentally
anchors.description: Aging or obsolete stock is found during physical counts or when space runs out; there is no systematic monitoring

anchors.label: Annual review
anchors.description: Inventory aging is reviewed once a year during financial close; by then, write-offs are large and options are limited

anchors.label: Periodic reports
anchors.description: Monthly aging reports are generated, but action depends on someone reviewing them and manually initiating disposition

anchors.label: Automated alerts
anchors.description: The system flags items approaching aging or obsolescence thresholds; defined workflows route them to the right team for action

anchors.label: Predictive lifecycle management
anchors.description: Analytics predict slow-moving risk at the point of purchase; dynamic pricing, promotions, or redeployment are triggered automatically before items become obsolete

text: When someone in your operations team needs approval — for a purchase, a schedule change, or a resource allocation — walk me through who they go to and how long it typically takes.
preAnswerContext: Approval workflows are one of the most common bottlenecks in operations. This question reveals whether your approval processes are designed for speed and accountability or whether they create queues that slow everything down. When approvals take days for routine decisions, it almost always signals that authority levels are unclear, systems do not support routing, or decision-makers are overloaded.

anchors.label: Unclear and slow
anchors.description: It is often unclear who can approve what; requests go to the wrong person, sit in email inboxes, and take days for routine matters

anchors.label: Person-dependent
anchors.description: Approvals route to specific individuals who may be unavailable; no delegation framework, so holidays or travel create backlogs

anchors.label: Defined but manual
anchors.description: Approval authority is documented and followed; requests are routed correctly but still require manual chasing and email-based tracking

anchors.label: System-routed
anchors.description: Approvals flow through a workflow system with defined escalation paths and SLAs; most routine approvals complete within hours

anchors.label: Auto-approved within guardrails
anchors.description: Routine decisions within defined parameters are auto-approved; only exceptions require human review, and those are fast-tracked with full context

text: How many operational decisions in your team require a human to look at data, make a judgment, and then manually enter or communicate the decision?
preAnswerContext: This question quantifies the manual decision load on your operations team. Every decision that requires a human to pull data, interpret it, and act on it is a candidate for either automation or decision support. A high count does not automatically mean automation is the answer — sometimes the process itself needs redesigning first — but it maps the territory of manual effort.

anchors.label: Almost everything
anchors.description: The vast majority of operational decisions require manual human judgment, even routine ones; there is no decision automation

anchors.label: Most decisions
anchors.description: Standard operating procedures exist but still require someone to check conditions and execute steps manually each time

anchors.label: Mix of manual and automated
anchors.description: Some routine decisions are automated (e.g., reorder triggers), but most non-trivial decisions require human review

anchors.label: Mostly automated
anchors.description: Routine decisions are automated with rules; humans handle exceptions and strategic decisions, supported by decision-ready dashboards

anchors.label: Humans for exceptions only
anchors.description: The vast majority of operational decisions are automated or AI-assisted; human involvement is reserved for genuinely novel situations and strategic choices

text: How do you track and manage the status of operational workflows — for example, an order from receipt through to delivery, or a maintenance request from submission through to completion?
preAnswerContext: Workflow visibility is the connective tissue of operational execution. This question reveals whether work items can be tracked through their lifecycle or whether they disappear into a black hole between handoffs. Poor workflow visibility is both a symptom of siloed systems and a cause of exception handling failures — you cannot manage what you cannot see.

anchors.label: No tracking
anchors.description: Work items are not systematically tracked; status is determined by asking people directly, and things regularly fall through the cracks

anchors.label: Spreadsheet tracking
anchors.description: Someone maintains a spreadsheet or whiteboard with status updates, but it is often out of date and not accessible to everyone

anchors.label: System-tracked per stage
anchors.description: Each stage of the workflow is tracked in its own system, but there is no end-to-end view and handoffs between stages are manual

anchors.label: End-to-end visibility
anchors.description: A workflow management system tracks items from start to finish; stakeholders can check status at any point without asking someone

anchors.label: Intelligent workflow management
anchors.description: Full end-to-end tracking with predictive SLA management; the system proactively identifies bottlenecks and suggests resequencing or reallocation

text: When something goes wrong in operations — a machine breaks, a shipment is delayed, a quality issue emerges — what happens in the first 30 minutes?
preAnswerContext: The first 30 minutes of an operational exception reveal your organisation's exception handling maturity. Whether people know who to call, where to look, and what decisions they can make without escalation determines whether small problems stay small or cascade. This question tests both the process design and the information infrastructure that supports rapid response.

anchors.label: Panic and improvisation
anchors.description: There is no standard response protocol; people scramble, make phone calls, and figure it out on the fly every time

anchors.label: Escalate to a person
anchors.description: Problems are escalated to a specific manager or expert who handles them personally; no documented playbook exists

anchors.label: Defined escalation path
anchors.description: An escalation process exists with clear roles; most people know who to contact, but the response is still largely manual

anchors.label: Playbook-driven response
anchors.description: Standard exception types have documented response playbooks; initial triage and containment steps are followed consistently

anchors.label: Automated detection and response
anchors.description: Exceptions are detected automatically by monitoring systems; response playbooks are triggered immediately with pre-populated context, and the right people are notified with decision-ready information

text: After an operational incident is resolved, does your team conduct a structured review to prevent recurrence — and do those lessons actually change anything?
preAnswerContext: Post-incident learning is where operational excellence is built or lost. Many organisations conduct root cause analyses after major incidents but fail to close the loop — the same types of failures recur because insights do not translate into process changes, training updates, or system improvements. This question tests the completeness of your learning cycle.

anchors.label: No review
anchors.description: Incidents are resolved and forgotten; no post-mortem, no documentation, the same issues recur regularly

anchors.label: Major incidents only
anchors.description: Serious incidents get a review, but findings are documented in a report that is filed and rarely referenced again

anchors.label: Reviews happen, actions lag
anchors.description: Post-incident reviews are conducted regularly with root cause analysis, but follow-through on corrective actions is inconsistent

anchors.label: Closed-loop learning
anchors.description: Reviews produce specific corrective actions that are tracked to completion; updated procedures are communicated and trained

anchors.label: Systematic prevention
anchors.description: Incident data is analysed for patterns across events; preventive measures are implemented proactively, and the knowledge base is continuously updated and accessible

text: How does your team currently predict which operational problems are likely to happen next week — or do you find out when they happen?
preAnswerContext: The shift from reactive to predictive operations is one of the clearest markers of operational maturity. This question reveals whether your organisation can see problems coming or is perpetually fighting fires. True predictive capability requires both the right data foundation and the analytical capability to use it — making this a strong test of AI candidacy in operations.

anchors.label: Purely reactive
anchors.description: The team discovers problems when they happen; there is no forward-looking capability and every day brings surprises

anchors.label: Experience-based intuition
anchors.description: Veteran staff can sense when trouble is coming based on patterns they have seen before, but this is not codified or shared

anchors.label: Leading indicator monitoring
anchors.description: Some leading indicators are tracked (e.g., machine hours, order backlog) but interpretation and action are manual and inconsistent

anchors.label: Statistical early warning
anchors.description: Statistical models or control charts identify abnormal patterns; alerts are generated when metrics drift beyond acceptable ranges

anchors.label: Predictive and prescriptive
anchors.description: Machine learning models predict specific likely failures or bottlenecks with timing estimates, and recommend specific preventive actions for each

text: From the moment a customer order is confirmed, how long does it take for them to receive what they ordered — and how does that compare to what you promised?
preAnswerContext: Order-to-delivery time is the customer-facing measure of your operational effectiveness. This question tests both the absolute speed of fulfilment and the reliability of your commitments. A gap between promised and actual delivery times reveals either planning inaccuracy, execution bottlenecks, or both — and tells us where the constraint lies.

anchors.label: Unpredictable
anchors.description: Delivery times vary wildly; promises are often missed by days or weeks, and the team cannot reliably predict when an order will ship

anchors.label: Frequently late
anchors.description: A standard lead time is quoted but missed regularly (more than 30% of orders); the team works around delays reactively

anchors.label: Mostly on time
anchors.description: 70-85% of orders are delivered within the promised window; delays are known and managed but not systematically eliminated

anchors.label: Reliable
anchors.description: On-time delivery exceeds 90%; lead times are accurate, and the team proactively communicates when exceptions occur

anchors.label: Best-in-class
anchors.description: On-time delivery exceeds 97%; lead times are continuously optimised, and customers receive proactive updates throughout the fulfilment process

text: Where in your fulfilment process does work most often get stuck waiting — waiting for information, waiting for a person, waiting for a system, or waiting for materials?
preAnswerContext: Wait time is the enemy of fulfilment speed, and knowing where it accumulates is the first step to eliminating it. This question identifies the dominant bottleneck type in your operations — whether it is information, people, technology, or materials — which directly determines the right intervention tier. An information bottleneck calls for integration; a people bottleneck calls for automation.

anchors.label: Waiting is pervasive
anchors.description: Work regularly stalls for hours or days at multiple points; nobody has mapped where the queues form or why

anchors.label: Known bottlenecks, no fix
anchors.description: Everyone knows where work gets stuck, but the root causes have not been addressed; workarounds are the norm

anchors.label: Bottlenecks mapped
anchors.description: Wait times are measured and the main bottlenecks are identified; some have been reduced, but significant queues remain

anchors.label: Actively managed
anchors.description: Flow metrics are monitored continuously; bottlenecks are systematically addressed through process redesign or system improvements

anchors.label: Continuously optimised
anchors.description: Real-time flow analytics identify emerging bottlenecks before they cause delays; automatic load balancing and routing keep work moving

text: If you wanted to cut your average fulfilment time in half, what would be the single biggest barrier — and is it a people problem, a process problem, or a technology problem?
preAnswerContext: This aspirational question calibrates the client's understanding of their own constraints and their ambition level. The barrier they name — and how they categorise it — reveals both their diagnostic clarity and where they see the greatest leverage. It also surfaces whether they have already identified the intervention type or whether the diagnostic needs to guide them there.

anchors.label: Cannot articulate
anchors.description: The team cannot identify a single biggest barrier; the problem feels too large and interconnected to decompose

anchors.label: Vague category
anchors.description: A general category is named (e.g., 'we need better systems') but there is no specific diagnosis or understanding of root cause

anchors.label: Specific barrier identified
anchors.description: A clear bottleneck is named with reasonable understanding of why it exists, but no plan to address it is in motion

anchors.label: Barrier understood and planned
anchors.description: The constraint is well understood, root causes are diagnosed, and an improvement initiative is underway with measurable targets

anchors.label: Continuous improvement cycle
anchors.description: Constraints are systematically identified, addressed, and re-evaluated in a continuous improvement loop; the next barrier is always being worked on

text: How does your quality control process work today — at what points do you check, how do you check, and what happens when something fails?
preAnswerContext: Quality control is where process discipline becomes tangible. This question reveals whether quality is designed into your operations or bolted on as an inspection step. The structure of your QC process — how many checkpoints, how data is captured, and how failures are handled — directly indicates whether you have the foundation for analytics and automation or need process redesign first.

anchors.label: Informal or absent
anchors.description: Quality checks are ad hoc or happen only when a customer complains; there is no defined inspection process or criteria

anchors.label: Final inspection only
anchors.description: Quality is checked at the end of the process; defects found at this stage are costly to fix and sometimes ship to customers anyway

anchors.label: Multi-point checks
anchors.description: Quality checkpoints exist at multiple stages; inspections follow defined criteria, but data capture is manual and inconsistent

anchors.label: Integrated and data-driven
anchors.description: Quality checks are embedded throughout the process with digital data capture; trends are monitored and feed into process improvement

anchors.label: Predictive quality
anchors.description: In-process sensors and analytics predict quality issues before they occur; the process self-adjusts to maintain quality parameters

text: When a quality issue or compliance requirement changes — a new regulation, a customer specification update, or an internal standard revision — how does that change flow into your day-to-day operations?
preAnswerContext: Change propagation in quality and compliance reveals whether your operations can adapt to new requirements quickly and completely or whether changes get lost between policy documents and shop floor behaviour. A slow or incomplete propagation cycle is both a compliance risk and a process maturity indicator.

anchors.label: Informally communicated
anchors.description: Changes are mentioned in meetings or sent via email; there is no tracking of whether everyone received, understood, and implemented the change

anchors.label: Document updated
anchors.description: A procedure document is updated and distributed, but there is no verification that people have read it or that processes have actually changed

anchors.label: Trained and acknowledged
anchors.description: Changes trigger a training or briefing process; affected staff acknowledge receipt, but compliance with the new standard is not systematically verified

anchors.label: Implemented and verified
anchors.description: Changes are rolled out through a structured change management process with updated checklists, training, and audit verification

anchors.label: Automated compliance
anchors.description: Regulatory and specification changes are mapped to operational controls automatically; system-enforced guardrails ensure compliance without relying on individual memory or discipline

text: How much of your quality and compliance data is captured digitally at the point of activity versus reconstructed later from paper forms, memory, or batch entry?
preAnswerContext: The gap between where quality data is created and where it is recorded determines its reliability and usefulness. Data captured after the fact — transcribed from paper, entered in batches, or recalled from memory — is inherently less trustworthy and less timely. This question directly tests your data capture maturity, which is a prerequisite for any analytics or automation layer on top of quality processes.

anchors.label: Paper-based
anchors.description: Quality data is recorded on paper forms and may or may not be entered into a system later; much of it is never digitised

anchors.label: Batch entry
anchors.description: Paper records are entered into a digital system daily or weekly; the delay means data is not available for real-time decisions

anchors.label: Mixed capture
anchors.description: Some quality data is captured digitally at the point of activity; other data still relies on paper or delayed entry

anchors.label: Mostly digital at source
anchors.description: Most quality checks use digital capture devices or mobile apps at the point of activity; paper is used only for edge cases

anchors.label: Fully automated capture
anchors.description: Quality data is captured automatically through sensors, scanners, or integrated systems with zero manual data entry; available instantly for analysis and action

text: Imagine you could see every quality incident, near-miss, and compliance deviation from the past year on a single screen — what patterns do you think you would find, and can you see that view today?
preAnswerContext: This question tests both the aspiration for quality intelligence and the current reality. Organisations that can already see cross-incident patterns are ready for advanced analytics; those that cannot yet aggregate their quality data need foundational data capture and integration work first. The patterns they imagine often reveal where they suspect systemic issues but lack the data to confirm them.

anchors.label: No aggregated view
anchors.description: Quality data is scattered across spreadsheets, paper files, and different systems; no single view exists and patterns are invisible

anchors.label: Partial visibility
anchors.description: Some quality data is aggregated in reports, but gaps in coverage and consistency make pattern identification unreliable

anchors.label: Retrospective reporting
anchors.description: Quarterly or annual quality reports aggregate incident data and surface major trends, but the analysis is manual and delayed

anchors.label: Dashboard with trends
anchors.description: A quality dashboard provides near-real-time visibility into incidents and deviations; trend analysis is available and reviewed regularly

anchors.label: Predictive quality intelligence
anchors.description: Advanced analytics identify emerging quality patterns, correlate incidents across domains, and predict where the next issues are likely to emerge

text: Walk me through what happens when an invoice arrives from a supplier — who touches it, what systems does it pass through, and how long does it typically take from receipt to payment?
preAnswerContext: This question maps the end-to-end accounts payable workflow. A fragmented or heavily manual process here is one of the strongest signals for automation readiness in finance. Your answer helps us determine whether the bottleneck is process design, system integration, or approval latency.

anchors.label: Fully manual
anchors.description: Invoices arrive on paper or email, are manually keyed into a system, and routed via email or physical signature for approval

anchors.label: Partially digitised
anchors.description: Invoices are entered into an ERP but matching, coding, and approval routing are mostly manual steps

anchors.label: Structured but slow
anchors.description: A defined AP workflow exists with some automation, but exceptions and approvals still create delays of days or weeks

anchors.label: Mostly automated
anchors.description: Most invoices are auto-matched to POs and auto-coded; only exceptions require manual intervention

anchors.label: Touchless processing
anchors.description: Straight-through processing for the majority of invoices with real-time matching, auto-approval for routine items, and exception-only human review

text: How does your team currently track and follow up on overdue receivables? Is there a systematic process, or does it depend on individual effort?
preAnswerContext: Collections effectiveness is a direct indicator of process maturity in finance. When follow-up is ad hoc, cash flow suffers and bad debt risk increases. This answer reveals whether receivables management is a designed process or a reactive effort.

anchors.label: No systematic follow-up
anchors.description: Overdue invoices are noticed ad hoc; follow-up depends entirely on individual memory or periodic manual checks

anchors.label: Spreadsheet-based tracking
anchors.description: Someone maintains a spreadsheet of overdue accounts but the process is inconsistent and reactive

anchors.label: Defined escalation process
anchors.description: There is a documented dunning process with defined touchpoints, but execution varies by team member

anchors.label: System-driven reminders
anchors.description: The ERP or billing system generates aging reports and automated reminders at defined intervals

anchors.label: Predictive and proactive
anchors.description: Collections are prioritised by risk scoring, with automated outreach for routine cases and human intervention focused on high-value or high-risk accounts

text: When a payment discrepancy appears — a short payment, a duplicate, or an unmatched credit note — how quickly does your team identify it, and what does the resolution process look like?
preAnswerContext: Payment discrepancy resolution is a high-frequency, high-effort task in most finance teams. The speed and consistency of resolution reveals both data quality and process design maturity. Slow resolution here often masks deeper integration or data capture problems.

anchors.label: Discovered late
anchors.description: Discrepancies surface days or weeks later, often during reconciliation, and require extensive manual investigation across systems

anchors.label: Identified but slow to resolve
anchors.description: Discrepancies are caught within a few days but resolving them requires chasing information across departments or systems

anchors.label: Caught promptly
anchors.description: Matching reports flag discrepancies within a day but resolution still requires manual cross-referencing and communication

anchors.label: Mostly automated detection
anchors.description: System-level matching catches most discrepancies automatically; resolution workflows are defined for common types

anchors.label: Real-time resolution
anchors.description: Discrepancies are detected at the point of entry, auto-classified by type, and most are resolved through automated rules with audit trail

text: If I asked three different people on your AP or AR team how they handle the same type of transaction, would I get the same answer?
preAnswerContext: Process consistency is a prerequisite for any form of automation or analytics. If the same transaction type is handled differently by different people, it signals that the process exists in people's heads rather than in a designed system. This is a strong indicator of the 'dark process' pattern.

anchors.label: Completely inconsistent
anchors.description: Each person has their own approach; there is no documented standard and outcomes vary significantly

anchors.label: Loosely similar
anchors.description: People follow a rough common approach but with significant personal variation in steps, timing, and documentation

anchors.label: Documented but not enforced
anchors.description: SOPs exist on paper but adherence varies; new team members learn by shadowing rather than from documentation

anchors.label: Mostly consistent
anchors.description: System-enforced workflows ensure the core process is followed; minor variations exist in edge case handling

anchors.label: Fully standardised
anchors.description: Workflows are system-enforced, exceptions are logged and reviewed, and process adherence is measured and managed

text: Describe what your month-end close process looks like from day one to final sign-off. How many calendar days does it typically take, and where do the delays concentrate?
preAnswerContext: Close cycle time is one of the most diagnostic metrics in finance. A slow close almost always reveals upstream data problems, manual reconciliation burdens, or approval bottlenecks. This answer helps us pinpoint whether the issue is process, data, or people.

anchors.label: Extended and chaotic
anchors.description: Close takes 15+ business days with significant firefighting, last-minute adjustments, and unclear ownership of tasks

anchors.label: Long but structured
anchors.description: Close takes 10-15 days with a checklist but many manual steps, dependencies on key individuals, and recurring delays

anchors.label: Defined and manageable
anchors.description: Close completes in 7-10 days with a structured calendar; most delays are predictable and relate to specific bottlenecks

anchors.label: Efficient
anchors.description: Close completes in 5-7 days with automated sub-processes, clear task ownership, and exception-based management

anchors.label: Accelerated
anchors.description: Close completes in under 5 days with continuous accounting practices, automated reconciliations, and real-time visibility into status

text: How much of your management reporting is produced automatically from source systems versus manually assembled in spreadsheets?
preAnswerContext: The ratio of automated to manual reporting reveals the true state of your data infrastructure. Heavy spreadsheet dependence indicates either system gaps, integration failures, or trust deficits in official data sources. This directly feeds the 'data trust deficit' and 'siloed systems' diagnostic patterns.

anchors.label: Almost entirely manual
anchors.description: Reports are built from scratch in spreadsheets each period, pulling data manually from multiple sources

anchors.label: Mostly manual
anchors.description: Some data is exported from systems but assembly, formatting, and commentary are manual spreadsheet work

anchors.label: Mixed
anchors.description: Core financial statements are system-generated but management packs, KPI dashboards, and ad hoc reports are manual

anchors.label: Mostly automated
anchors.description: Standard reports are auto-generated from integrated sources; manual effort is limited to commentary and exception analysis

anchors.label: Fully automated
anchors.description: All recurring reports are auto-generated and distributed; finance team focuses on analysis and insight rather than report production

text: When a senior stakeholder asks for a financial number that isn't in the standard pack — say, profitability by customer segment or cost per transaction — how long does it take to produce, and how confident are you in the answer?
preAnswerContext: Ad hoc reporting agility is a litmus test for data architecture. If answering a reasonable business question takes days of manual effort, the underlying data model is not fit for purpose. This question surfaces both data accessibility and the gap between what the business needs and what finance can deliver.

anchors.label: Days to weeks
anchors.description: Answering requires pulling data from multiple systems, manual manipulation, and significant assumptions; confidence is low

anchors.label: A few days
anchors.description: The data exists somewhere but finding, combining, and validating it takes considerable effort

anchors.label: Within a day
anchors.description: Most data is accessible but combining it for non-standard views requires some manual work and assumptions

anchors.label: Within hours
anchors.description: A well-structured data model allows most reasonable questions to be answered quickly with high confidence

anchors.label: Self-service
anchors.description: Stakeholders can answer most questions themselves through dashboards or a well-governed data layer; finance validates rather than produces

text: What would it take for your finance team to produce a reliable flash close — a directionally accurate P&L within 48 hours of month end?
preAnswerContext: The flash close question reveals how far the organisation is from continuous accounting. The gap between current close time and a 48-hour flash close is one of the strongest indicators of automation and integration opportunity in finance.

anchors.label: Not feasible
anchors.description: Too many manual dependencies and data gaps; a flash close would be unreliable to the point of being misleading

anchors.label: Major effort required
anchors.description: Possible in theory but would require significant process change, system integration, and data quality improvement

anchors.label: Achievable with workarounds
anchors.description: Could produce a directionally accurate flash with known estimation methods, but it would require dedicated effort each month

anchors.label: Close to possible
anchors.description: Most components are in place; a few specific bottlenecks would need to be resolved

anchors.label: Already happening
anchors.description: A reliable flash close or near-real-time financial view is already produced routinely

text: How are bank reconciliations performed today? Walk me through the process for your highest-volume account — who does it, how long does it take, and what percentage of items require manual investigation?
preAnswerContext: Bank reconciliation is a canonical automation target. The ratio of auto-matched to manually investigated items is a direct measure of data quality and integration maturity. High manual investigation rates almost always indicate either poor transaction categorisation upstream or disconnected systems.

anchors.label: Fully manual
anchors.description: Reconciliation is done line-by-line in spreadsheets; the majority of items require manual matching and investigation

anchors.label: Partially aided
anchors.description: Some system support for matching but 40%+ of items still require manual investigation and adjustment

anchors.label: Semi-automated
anchors.description: Auto-matching handles routine items but 20-40% still need manual review; the process takes several days

anchors.label: Mostly automated
anchors.description: Auto-matching resolves 80%+ of items; manual effort focuses on genuine exceptions; completed within a day

anchors.label: Continuous reconciliation
anchors.description: Near-real-time matching with exception-only review; reconciliation is a continuous process rather than a periodic event

text: How many different types of reconciliation does your finance team perform monthly — intercompany, sub-ledger to GL, inventory, accruals — and which ones consistently cause the most issues?
preAnswerContext: The volume and pain distribution of reconciliation types maps directly to integration architecture gaps. Consistently problematic reconciliations point to specific system boundaries where data integrity breaks down. This is one of the most reliable signals for integration investment priorities.

anchors.label: Uncharted
anchors.description: The full reconciliation landscape is not documented; different people handle different reconciliations with no overall view

anchors.label: Known but painful
anchors.description: The reconciliation types are understood but most are manual, several consistently cause delays, and the root causes persist

anchors.label: Managed unevenly
anchors.description: Some reconciliations are well-handled while others are persistent pain points; prioritisation is ad hoc

anchors.label: Systematically managed
anchors.description: All reconciliation types are documented, assigned, and tracked; problem areas are identified and being addressed

anchors.label: Optimised
anchors.description: Reconciliation landscape is fully mapped, most are automated, and residual manual reconciliations have documented root cause remediation plans

text: When your internal or external auditors ask for supporting documentation, how quickly can your team produce it, and how often does the initial response satisfy the request?
preAnswerContext: Audit responsiveness is a proxy for data accessibility and documentation quality. Slow or incomplete responses indicate that supporting evidence lives in people's heads, personal files, or disconnected systems rather than in a structured, searchable format.

anchors.label: Painful and slow
anchors.description: Audit requests trigger days of searching through emails, files, and systems; responses frequently require multiple follow-ups

anchors.label: Eventually found
anchors.description: Documentation exists but is scattered; producing it requires effort and the first response often needs supplementation

anchors.label: Reasonably responsive
anchors.description: Most documentation is accessible within a day; some areas consistently require more effort than others

anchors.label: Well-organised
anchors.description: Documentation is structured and findable; most audit requests are satisfied on first response within hours

anchors.label: Audit-ready at all times
anchors.description: Supporting documentation is continuously maintained, centrally accessible, and audit requests are served from a single source of truth

text: How confident are you that the data flowing between your sub-ledgers, general ledger, and reporting layer is accurate and complete — and what gives you that confidence or doubt?
preAnswerContext: Confidence in data flow integrity is foundational. If the finance team does not trust the numbers flowing through their own systems, every downstream process — from reporting to forecasting to compliance — is compromised. Low confidence here is a strong foundation-gap signal.

anchors.label: Low confidence
anchors.description: Regular discrepancies between sub-ledgers and GL; the team spends significant time investigating data integrity issues

anchors.label: Conditional confidence
anchors.description: Core transactions flow reliably but specific areas (intercompany, accruals, allocations) regularly require manual correction

anchors.label: Moderate confidence
anchors.description: Data is generally accurate but the team performs manual checks each period because they have been surprised before

anchors.label: High confidence
anchors.description: Automated controls and reconciliations provide reliable data flow; exceptions are rare and quickly identified

anchors.label: Verified confidence
anchors.description: Continuous data quality monitoring with automated alerts; the team trusts the numbers because they have evidence, not just belief

text: Describe how your annual budgeting process works. How many iterations does it typically go through, how many people are involved in assembling it, and what tools do they use?
preAnswerContext: Budgeting process design reveals both organisational complexity and tool maturity. Multi-iteration spreadsheet-based budgeting is one of the most common and most costly manual processes in finance. Understanding the current state is essential for sizing the automation and analytics opportunity.

anchors.label: Spreadsheet chaos
anchors.description: Budgeting is entirely spreadsheet-based with dozens of files, multiple email-based iterations, and no version control

anchors.label: Structured spreadsheets
anchors.description: Templates exist but the process still relies on emailing spreadsheets, manual consolidation, and several rounds of revision

anchors.label: Partially systematic
anchors.description: A budgeting tool or structured process exists but significant manual effort is still required for data gathering and consolidation

anchors.label: Tool-supported
anchors.description: A dedicated planning tool handles most of the workflow; iterations are managed systematically with clear version control

anchors.label: Integrated planning
