anchors.description: The team sometimes discusses what went wrong, but insights are not captured or applied to future estimates

anchors.label: Regular retrospectives
anchors.description: Sprint retrospectives address estimation issues, but root causes often repeat because systemic fixes are not implemented

anchors.label: Root cause analysis
anchors.description: Significant overruns trigger structured analysis that identifies root causes and produces specific process improvements

anchors.label: Continuous calibration
anchors.description: Estimation accuracy is tracked over time; the team uses historical data to calibrate estimates and address systemic causes of variance

text: How do you decide what goes into the next sprint versus what gets deprioritised?
preAnswerContext: This question reveals whether prioritisation is principled or political. Most organisations claim to prioritise by business value, but in practice the loudest stakeholder or the most recent customer escalation wins. A low score here signals that prioritisation decisions are not traceable to clear criteria, which makes it impossible to explain to stakeholders why their request was deprioritised. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Prioritisation Rigour' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Loudest voice wins
anchors.description: Prioritisation is driven by whoever pushes hardest; there are no agreed criteria and the process feels arbitrary

anchors.label: Informal judgement
anchors.description: A product manager makes the call based on their judgement, but the reasoning is not transparent or documented

anchors.label: Criteria exist
anchors.description: Prioritisation criteria are defined (value, effort, risk) but are applied inconsistently or overridden by stakeholder pressure

anchors.label: Structured framework
anchors.description: A consistent prioritisation framework is applied; stakeholders can see why items are ranked as they are

anchors.label: Data-driven prioritisation
anchors.description: Prioritisation combines quantitative signals (usage data, revenue impact, customer demand) with strategic alignment scoring, and decisions are transparent

text: When a strategic initiative competes with customer requests and technical debt for roadmap space, how is that tension resolved?
preAnswerContext: This question probes the maturity of your roadmap governance. The three-way tension between strategic bets, customer demands, and technical health is the defining challenge of product management. Organisations that lack a clear framework for resolving this tension oscillate between reactive customer-driven development and ambitious but ungrounded strategic initiatives. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Roadmap Governance' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Crisis-driven
anchors.description: Whatever is most urgent wins; there is no standing framework for balancing strategic work, customer requests, and technical debt

anchors.label: Pendulum swing
anchors.description: The organisation swings between periods of customer-driven work and strategic pushes, with technical debt perpetually deferred

anchors.label: Allocation targets
anchors.description: Rough allocation targets exist (e.g., 60/20/20) but they are not consistently enforced and shift under pressure

anchors.label: Balanced governance
anchors.description: A product governance process explicitly balances all three categories with defined criteria and regular review

anchors.label: Integrated portfolio management
anchors.description: Strategic, customer, and technical investments are managed as a portfolio with clear trade-off criteria, regular rebalancing, and transparent decision records

text: How far ahead does your product roadmap extend, and how confident are you in what it says beyond the next quarter?
preAnswerContext: This question calibrates the planning horizon and its reliability. A roadmap that extends 12 months but is only reliable for 4 weeks is not a planning tool -- it is a fiction that creates false expectations. Conversely, a team that cannot articulate direction beyond the current sprint lacks strategic coherence. The answer reveals whether your planning horizon matches your organisational needs. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Planning Horizon' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Sprint-only view
anchors.description: The team only knows what is in the current sprint; there is no roadmap beyond the immediate work

anchors.label: Quarter at best
anchors.description: A rough quarterly plan exists but changes so frequently that stakeholders have stopped trusting it

anchors.label: Rolling quarterly
anchors.description: A quarterly roadmap is maintained with reasonable confidence; beyond that, themes exist but specifics are uncertain

anchors.label: Themed horizon
anchors.description: A 6-12 month roadmap shows committed items near-term and strategic themes further out, with clear confidence levels

anchors.label: Strategic product plan
anchors.description: A multi-horizon roadmap connects near-term delivery to long-term product strategy, with appropriate granularity at each level and regular stakeholder alignment

text: When a major stakeholder requests a change to the roadmap, what process ensures this does not derail existing commitments?
preAnswerContext: This question tests change management discipline in product planning. Uncontrolled scope injection is one of the most common causes of delivery failure. If any senior stakeholder can redirect the roadmap without a formal trade-off discussion, the team is effectively operating without a plan. A low score reveals missing governance that allows disruption disguised as agility. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Scope Governance' diagnosis.

anchors.label: No protection
anchors.description: Senior stakeholders can and do redirect the team at any time; existing commitments are routinely abandoned without discussion

anchors.label: Product manager absorbs
anchors.description: The product manager tries to accommodate requests by squeezing them in, leading to overcommitment and burnout

anchors.label: Informal negotiation
anchors.description: Trade-off discussions happen but are informal; the outcome depends on the relative authority of the requester

anchors.label: Formal change process
anchors.description: Roadmap changes go through a defined process with impact assessment and explicit trade-off decisions

anchors.label: Governed flexibility
anchors.description: A clear change governance process balances responsiveness with commitment protection; all changes are recorded with rationale and impact

text: How does your engineering team know the size of the technical debt they're carrying?
preAnswerContext: This question diagnoses visibility into technical debt -- one of the most common invisible costs in product organisations. If the engineering team cannot quantify or even describe their technical debt, it is impossible to make informed trade-off decisions about when to address it. A low score means debt is accumulating silently, slowing down every future delivery without anyone being able to explain why. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Debt Visibility' diagnosis.

anchors.label: No visibility
anchors.description: Technical debt is not tracked; engineers know it exists from experience but there is no inventory, measurement, or shared language for it

anchors.label: Anecdotal awareness
anchors.description: Engineers can point to specific areas of concern, but there is no systematic way to assess the overall debt level

anchors.label: Informal tracking
anchors.description: Some teams maintain tech debt backlogs, but prioritisation and measurement are inconsistent across the organisation

anchors.label: Catalogued and measured
anchors.description: Technical debt is catalogued with estimated impact; the team can articulate the cost of inaction for major debt items

anchors.label: Quantified debt management
anchors.description: Technical debt is continuously measured through code quality metrics and velocity trends, with a dedicated allocation for reduction and clear ROI tracking

text: When technical debt slows down a feature delivery, how does the team make that cost visible to business stakeholders?
preAnswerContext: This question reveals whether technical debt has a voice in business conversations or remains an invisible engineering concern. When debt cannot be translated into business impact (slower delivery, higher costs, increased risk), it will always lose the prioritisation battle against visible customer requests. A low score means the engineering team is carrying a burden that business leaders do not understand. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Debt Communication' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Invisible to business
anchors.description: Business stakeholders have no idea that technical debt is slowing down delivery; engineering absorbs the cost silently

anchors.label: Engineering complaints
anchors.description: Engineers raise debt concerns but cannot translate them into business language; the conversation does not resonate

anchors.label: Occasional escalation
anchors.description: Major debt items are escalated when they cause visible problems, but proactive communication is rare

anchors.label: Regular reporting
anchors.description: Technical debt impact is reported to business stakeholders regularly in terms they understand (delivery speed, risk, cost)

anchors.label: Business-integrated view
anchors.description: Technical debt is included in business planning as a quantified cost; trade-off decisions between debt reduction and feature development are made collaboratively

text: How confident is your team that the current architecture can support 3x the current load without a major rewrite?
preAnswerContext: This question surfaces architectural risk and scalability readiness. Organisations that have not tested or modelled their architecture under growth scenarios are carrying hidden risk that typically materialises at the worst possible time -- during a growth surge. A low score combined with growth ambitions signals an urgent need for architectural assessment. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Scalability Readiness' diagnosis.

anchors.label: No confidence
anchors.description: Nobody has tested or modelled scalability; the team suspects significant work would be needed but cannot quantify it

anchors.label: Known limitations
anchors.description: The team knows specific bottlenecks that would break under 3x load, but a comprehensive assessment has not been done

anchors.label: Partially assessed
anchors.description: Some load testing has been done; critical paths are understood but not all components have been evaluated

anchors.label: Modelled and planned
anchors.description: Architecture has been assessed against growth scenarios with a clear plan for addressing known limitations

anchors.label: Designed for scale
anchors.description: Architecture is built with scalability in mind, regularly load-tested, and the team has high confidence in handling 3x growth with minimal changes

text: When a component or service needs to be replaced or upgraded, how well does the team understand its dependencies and blast radius?
preAnswerContext: This question probes architectural documentation and dependency management. If replacing a component feels risky because nobody fully understands what depends on it, the organisation has an architectural visibility problem. This creates a paralysis where teams avoid necessary improvements because the risk of unintended consequences is too high. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Dependency Visibility' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Unknown dependencies
anchors.description: Nobody has a complete picture of system dependencies; changes are made with fingers crossed and incidents are the discovery mechanism

anchors.label: Tribal knowledge
anchors.description: A few senior engineers know the dependency map from experience, but it is not documented or shared

anchors.label: Partial documentation
anchors.description: Some architectural documentation exists, but it is outdated and does not cover all integration points

anchors.label: Documented architecture
anchors.description: Up-to-date architecture diagrams and dependency maps exist; impact assessment is part of the change process

anchors.label: Automated dependency tracking
anchors.description: Dependencies are automatically discovered and documented; blast radius analysis is available before any change is made

text: When someone on the product team has an idea for improving something, what is their path from idea to experiment?
preAnswerContext: This question reveals whether your organisation has a functioning innovation pipeline or whether ideas die in the gap between inspiration and execution. A clear, low-friction path from idea to experiment is the hallmark of an innovative culture. A low score typically means that innovation is talked about but not structurally supported -- there is no time, no process, and no permission. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Innovation Pipeline' diagnosis.

anchors.label: No path
anchors.description: There is no mechanism for turning ideas into experiments; innovation depends entirely on individual initiative in spare time

anchors.label: Suggestion box
anchors.description: Ideas can be submitted but rarely progress; there is no allocated time, budget, or process for experimentation

anchors.label: Periodic hackathons
anchors.description: Innovation events (hackathons, innovation days) exist but are disconnected from the main product process

anchors.label: Structured experimentation
anchors.description: A defined process exists for proposing and running experiments with allocated time and clear success criteria

anchors.label: Continuous innovation
anchors.description: Experimentation is embedded in the product process with dedicated capacity, rapid validation cycles, and a clear path from experiment to production

text: How does your team run experiments to validate whether an idea will work before committing to a full build?
preAnswerContext: This question diagnoses experimentation discipline -- the ability to test hypotheses cheaply before investing in full development. Organisations that skip validation and jump straight to building are the ones most likely to ship features nobody wants. A low score signals either a cultural resistance to experimentation or missing infrastructure for rapid validation. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Validation Discipline' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Build first, validate later
anchors.description: The team builds features to completion and then sees whether they work; there is no validation step before full investment

anchors.label: Occasional prototyping
anchors.description: Some teams prototype before building, but this is not systematic and is often skipped under time pressure

anchors.label: MVP approach
anchors.description: The team generally tries to ship a minimal version first, but the definition of 'minimal' varies and true experiments are rare

anchors.label: Structured validation
anchors.description: A defined validation process exists: hypothesis, experiment design, success criteria, and decision point before full build

anchors.label: Rapid experimentation culture
anchors.description: The team routinely runs cheap experiments (prototypes, A/B tests, fake doors) with clear hypotheses and automated measurement before committing resources

text: When an experiment fails -- the hypothesis was wrong -- what happens to that learning?
preAnswerContext: This question tests whether your organisation treats failed experiments as valuable data or as waste. The most innovative organisations learn as much from failures as from successes, but only if those learnings are captured, shared, and accessible. A low score reveals either a culture that punishes failure or a process that does not capture learning -- both of which suppress innovation. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Learning from Failure' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Nothing happens
anchors.description: Failed experiments are quietly abandoned; there is no documentation of what was tested, what was learned, or why it failed

anchors.label: Informal conversation
anchors.description: The team discusses what happened but learnings are not documented and are quickly forgotten

anchors.label: Team-level learning
anchors.description: The team captures learnings from failed experiments, but this knowledge stays within the team and is not shared broadly

anchors.label: Shared learning repository
anchors.description: Experiment results -- both successes and failures -- are documented in a shared repository that other teams can access

anchors.label: Institutional learning system
anchors.description: All experiment outcomes are systematically captured, categorised, and actively referenced in future decision-making across the organisation

text: If you could eliminate one recurring bottleneck in your product development process tomorrow, what would it be and how would you know it was actually eliminated?
preAnswerContext: This is a calibration question that reveals both the most painful constraint in your product process and whether the organisation can measure improvement. The first part surfaces the top-of-mind pain point; the second part reveals whether measurement infrastructure exists to validate that an intervention worked. If they can name the bottleneck but not the metric, that is itself a diagnostic signal. This feeds the 'Product & Innovation -- Constraint Awareness' diagnosis.

anchors.label: Cannot identify or measure
anchors.description: The team struggles to name a specific bottleneck or has no way to measure whether any process change actually helps

anchors.label: Can name, cannot measure
anchors.description: The team can identify their biggest constraint but has no metrics or instrumentation to track improvement

anchors.label: Known and partially tracked
anchors.description: The bottleneck is well-known and some metrics exist, but measurement is manual and inconsistent

anchors.label: Identified with metrics
anchors.description: The constraint is clearly identified with specific metrics that would demonstrate improvement; some interventions are underway

anchors.label: Systematic constraint management
anchors.description: Key process bottlenecks are continuously identified, measured, and addressed through a structured improvement process with tracked outcomes
